title: Trinidadian stew chicken
subtitle: Chicken braised in burned sugar with Trinidadian seasonings
excerpt: Chicken pieces seasoned overnight in green seasoning and marinated, then browned in caramelised sugar and braised in a dark, aromatic sauce. The burned-sugar technique is central to the dish — the caramel forms the base of the sauce and gives it a bitter-sweet depth that cannot be replicated by browning in oil alone. Served with macaroni pie, rice and peas, and plantain at the Trinidadian Sunday table.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian stew chicken is a cornerstone of the Sunday lunch table. The burned-sugar browning technique is a defining characteristic of Trinidadian cooking, appearing in stew chicken, pelau, and other dishes. Green seasoning, a blended paste of herbs, garlic, and scotch bonnet, is the marinade base that distinguishes the Trinidadian version from Jamaican brown stew chicken.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes very well. The sauce deepens further on reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Double without changing the method. The burn-sugar step works the same at scale.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Overnight marinade produces noticeably better results. Minimum 4 hours.

content.text: The burned sugar step is the same as for pelau. Take the caster sugar to a very dark brown, almost at the point of smelling bitter, then add the chicken immediately. The chicken will produce a lot of steam and the sugar will seize; continue cooking over high heat until the sugar dissolves back into the chicken juices and a dark, sticky glaze forms.

Green seasoning note: Trinidadian green seasoning is a blended paste of coriander, culantro, spring onion, garlic, scotch bonnet, and thyme. If unavailable, blend together a handful of coriander, 4 spring onions, 3 cloves garlic, and half a scotch bonnet for a close approximation.

Combine the {{chicken-thigh}} with {{green-seasoning}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Cover and marinate for at least 4 hours. Heat a large heavy pot over high heat. Add the {{caster-sugar}} and swirl until it melts and turns very dark brown. Add the marinated chicken (it will spit). Stir and cook over high heat until the sugar has dissolved into the chicken juices, about 4 minutes. Remove the chicken and set aside.

In the same pot, add the {{onion}} and cook for 3 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{tomato}}, {{thyme}}, {{allspice}}, and {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole). Cook for 3 minutes. Return the chicken. Add 200 ml of water and stir. Reduce to a medium simmer, cover, and cook for 30 minutes. Remove the lid and cook for a further 10 minutes until the sauce has reduced to a thick, dark coating. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving.

items.cause: Sugar burned past the dark-brown stage
items.fix: Take the sugar to the point just before it smells acrid. Practice helps — discard and restart if the first attempt goes too dark

items.cause: Insufficient marinade time or heat too high during braise
items.fix: The 4-hour minimum is important. The green seasoning tenderises as well as flavours. Braise at medium, not a rolling boil

items.cause: Too much water or lid left on throughout
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 10 minutes. The sauce should reduce to a thick, dark coating that clings to the chicken

content.text: Stew chicken is the anchor of the Trinidadian Sunday lunch. It anchors a table that also includes macaroni pie, rice and peas or pelau, plantain, coleslaw, and provision (root vegetables). The burned-sugar technique is one of the most distinctive elements of Trinidadian cooking — it appears in every household regardless of ethnic background and is one of the flavour signatures that makes Trinidadian food immediately recognisable. In Britain the Trinidadian diaspora makes stew chicken as a Sunday staple and for every gathering and family occasion.
